vulnerability.title: Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing
vulnerability.description: The SELinux state should be set to at system boot time. In the file /etc/selinux/config, add or correct the following line to configure the system to boot into enforcing mode: SELINUX= Ensure that all files have correct SELinux labels by running: fixfiles onboot Then reboot the system.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_selinux_state='' if [ -e "/etc/selinux/config" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^SELINUX=/Id" "/etc/selinux/config" else touch "/etc/selinux/config" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/selinux/config" cp "/etc/selinux/config" "/etc/selinux/config.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "SELINUX=$var_selinux_state" >> "/etc/selinux/config" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/selinux/config.bak" fixfiles onboot else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: Changing the SELinux state to enforcing is a standard configuration change that does not lock out users or break critical services. It follows a typical STIG hardening pattern and is safe to apply.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Requires system reboot to take effect; A misconfiguration could temporarily reduce security enforcement

execution_details.stdout:
# This file controls the state of SELinux on the system.
# SELINUX= can take one of these three values:
#     enforcing - SELinux security policy is enforced.
#     permissive - SELinux prints warnings instead of enforcing.
#     disabled - No SELinux policy is loaded.
# See also:
# https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html/using_selinux/changing-selinux-states-and-modes_using-selinux#changing-selinux-modes-at-boot-time_changing-selinux-states-and-modes
#
# NOTE: Up to RHEL 8 release included, SELINUX=disabled would also
# fully disable SELinux during boot. If you need a system with SELinux
# fully disabled instead of SELinux running with no policy loaded, you
# need to pass selinux=0 to the kernel command line. You can use grubby
# to persistently set the bootloader to boot with selinux=0:
#
#    grubby --update-kernel ALL --args selinux=0
#
# To revert back to SELinux enabled:
#
#    grubby --update-kernel ALL --remove-args selinux
#
SELINUX=disabled
# SELINUXTYPE= can take one of these three values:
#     targeted - Targeted processes are protected,
#     mls - Multi Level Security protection.
SELINUXTYPE=targeted

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly sets SELINUX to enforcing and runs fixfiles to relabel. It will resolve the vulnerability.
concerns: Does not handle the case where /etc/selinux/config does not exist; will fail if the file is missing.
suggested_improvements:
Add a check to create /etc/selinux/config if it does not exist before attempting to modify it.
Use an explicit file write (e.g., printf 'SELINUX=enforcing\n' > /etc/selinux/config) after deleting any existing SELINUX lines to avoid accidental duplicate lines or trailing whitespace.
Consider verifying that SELINUXTYPE is present or defaulting to targeted if missing.
